planting.note: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in well-drained to moderately dry, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; roundleaf alumroot is one of the most drought-tolerant heucheras and thrives in lean, rocky soils on western slopes and canyon walls where other heucheras fail.

directions: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in well-drained to moderately dry, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; roundleaf alumroot is one of the most drought-tolerant heucheras and thrives in lean, rocky soils on western slopes and canyon walls where other heucheras fail. Heuchera cylindrica is a native of western mountain cliffs, rocky slopes, and open woodlands from British Columbia to California, with large, rounded, scalloped, maple-shaped leaves and distinctive, tall, slender, cylindrical spikes of greenish-white to cream flowers with no petals but showy creamy-tan calyces in late spring; it is the parent species of several garden hybrids. In the garden it is outstanding in dry shade, rocky walls, and xeric borders in zones 3–8 and is a useful companion to the more ornamental hybrid heucheras where its structural flower spikes provide textural interest.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; hairy alumroot is native to the rich woodlands and rock outcrops of the Appalachians and is better adapted to the humidity and summer heat of the mid-Atlantic and upper South than the Pacific Coast native species.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; hairy alumroot is native to the rich woodlands and rock outcrops of the Appalachians and is better adapted to the humidity and summer heat of the mid-Atlantic and upper South than the Pacific Coast native species. Heuchera pubescens is a distinctly hairy alumroot native to the central and southern Appalachian Mountain woodland zone, producing bold, shallowly lobed, softly hairy, medium-green leaves and taller-than-average panicles of small, whitish to greenish-pink flowers with hairy sepals and petals in late spring to early summer; it is a strong, reliable shade perennial for the East and mid-South that tolerates the heat and humidity of zone 7–8 better than many western heuchera cultivars. An excellent naturalizer for woodland gardens, it bridges the gap between showier garden hybrids and the purely ecological value of wild-collected plants.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; small-flowered alumroot is a Pacific Northwest native that tolerates deeper shade than most heucheras, making it useful as a shade groundcover under conifers where few perennials survive.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; small-flowered alumroot is a Pacific Northwest native that tolerates deeper shade than most heucheras, making it useful as a shade groundcover under conifers where few perennials survive. Heuchera micrantha is the Pacific Northwest native alumroot most closely associated with the famous 'Palace Purple' cultivar and the cascade of bronze- and purple-leafed garden heucheras that followed; the species itself has large, lobed, light-to-medium-green leaves with silver mottling and produces tall, open, airy panicles of tiny white flowers in late spring. It is native to moist, forested cliffs and shaded stream banks from British Columbia to northern California and is an important parent species in Heuchera hybridization programs; in the garden it is a tough, reliable shade perennial for the Pacific Northwest and is more tolerant of dry shade than most species once established.

planting.note: Plant container plants or divisions after last frost in well-drained, average to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; hairy phlox benefits from light shearing after bloom to maintain a compact, dense mat and encourage reblooming in fall.

directions: Plant container plants or divisions after last frost in well-drained, average to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; hairy phlox benefits from light shearing after bloom to maintain a compact, dense mat and encourage reblooming in fall. Phlox amoena is a low-growing, mat-forming native phlox of dry, open woodlands, forest edges, and rocky barrens of the southeastern United States from Virginia to Florida and west to Tennessee; it produces small, hairy, semi-evergreen leaves and a profuse display of small, pink to magenta-pink, five-petaled flowers with a characteristic lobed petal shape in early spring, often providing one of the earliest color displays in the native perennial garden. It is an excellent, low-maintenance native groundcover for slopes, rock gardens, and dry to average woodland edges in zones 5–9; more heat-tolerant than creeping phlox (P. subulata) and a good choice for the humid South.

planting.note: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; smooth phlox is adapted to moist, streamside woodland edges and is more tolerant of partial shade and consistent soil moisture than meadow phlox (P. maculata) or the dry-adapted P. carolina.

directions: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; smooth phlox is adapted to moist, streamside woodland edges and is more tolerant of partial shade and consistent soil moisture than meadow phlox (P. maculata) or the dry-adapted P. carolina. Phlox glaberrima is a smooth-stemmed native phlox of moist to wet lowland prairies, stream edges, and open forest glades throughout the southeastern and central United States, producing dense, rounded clusters of rich pink to magenta-pink flowers in late spring to early summer on unbranched to lightly branched upright stems; it is disease-resistant (less prone to powdery mildew than P. paniculata), moist-soil tolerant, and heat-hardy in zones 7–9. An outstanding native alternative to garden phlox for moist, sunny to partially shaded borders and rain gardens, supporting hummingbirds, swallowtails, and hummingbird hawk-moths.
